votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq logic, summing the ASCII values of uppercase letters (characters with ASCII codes between 65 and 90 inclusive).
The Python code correctly implements the logic of summing ASCII values of uppercase characters (codes 65-90), matching the Coq definition.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq logic, summing the ASCII values of characters whose ASCII codes are between 65 and 90 inclusive.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code: it iterates through each character, checks if the ASCII code is between 65 and 90 (uppercase letters), and if so adds the ASCII code to the total. The spec function checks equality with the computed sum. Both produce the same concrete results for all inputs.

The Python code correctly iterates through each character, checks if it's an uppercase letter (ASCII 65-90), and sums the ASCII codes of uppercase letters. This matches the Coq recursive function digitSum_fun which does the same: for each character, it adds the ASCII code if it's uppercase (65 <= code <= 90), otherwise adds 0. The spec in both cases checks if the given sum equals the computed result.

Both sum the code points of uppercase ASCII letters (65–90) and ignore others; spec checks equality accordingly. Behavior matches for all Coq-representable strings.
Both compute the sum of character codes for ASCII uppercase letters (A-Z) in the string; the spec is equality to this function, which the Python code matches.
Both sum the ASCII codes of uppercase A-Z (65..90) and ignore all other characters; Python ord and Coq nat_of_ascii align on ASCII, and the logic matches.
